schools.summary: God blesses Noah: 'and God blessed Noah and his sons, and said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth' — as God blessed Adam at the first creation (1:28), so now he blesses Noah and his sons at the renewal of the world (the blessing of the new humanity, God's favor resting on the family preserved through the flood, the renewal of the creation-blessing after the judgment); 'Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth' repeating the command given to Adam (1:28, Noah and his sons to multiply and refill the depopulated earth, the human race, reduced to eight souls, to increase and people the world again, the mandate of fruitfulness renewed upon the new head of mankind, through Noah's family the earth repeopled); 'replenish (fill) the earth' (the earth, emptied by the flood, to be filled again with men, the command both blessing and obligating, God's purpose to repopulate the world through Noah, Noah as the second father of mankind, after Adam, from whom all nations would spring) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And God blessed Noah and his sons, and said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth
God blessing Noah and renewing the command to replenish the earth
the creation-mandate given to Adam (1:28) renewed on the new head of mankind
the emptied world to be repeopled through Noah

schools.summary: The fear of man on the creatures: 'and the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth, and upon every fowl of the air, upon all that moveth upon the earth, and upon all the fishes of the sea; into your hand are they delivered' — God placing the fear and dread of man upon all the creatures (man's dominion, 1:28, renewed, but now in a new form, through fear, the creatures, which might have grown dangerous to the diminished human race, made to dread man, the renewed lordship of man over the animals); the dominion now exercised through 'fear and dread' (a harsher relation than the original, the creatures now wild and apt to harm, after the fall and flood, man's rule over the animals maintained by their instinctive fear of him, God's provision to protect the few survivors and their descendants from the beasts); 'into your hand are they delivered' (the creatures given into man's hand, for his use, including food, v.3, God delivering the animals to man's power and disposal, the renewed grant of dominion, now extending to the use of animals for food, man's lordship over the creatures confirmed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth... into your hand are they delivered
the fear of man placed upon the creatures, delivered into his hand
man's dominion renewed, now exercised through the creatures' fear
the creatures given to man's use and power

schools.summary: Animals granted for food: 'every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things' — God now granting man the animals for food (whereas at the first man was given the herbs and fruits, 1:29, now, after the flood, the flesh of living creatures added to his diet, the express grant of animal food to Noah and his descendants, the enlarged provision for man); 'even as the green herb have I given you all things' (as God had given the green herb, the vegetable food, at the first, 1:29, so now he gives all things, the animals also, for food, the comparison marking the enlargement of the grant, man now eating of the creatures as freely as of the herbs, God's gracious provision for the renewed humanity); the question of whether flesh was eaten before (some holding animal food first permitted here, after the flood, others that it was eaten before but now expressly sanctioned, whichever, God here granting the use of animals for food, with the one restriction of the blood, v.4) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things
the grant of animals for food
the provision enlarged from herbs (1:29) to all things, including flesh
God's gracious provision for the renewed humanity

schools.summary: The prohibition of blood: 'but flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat' — though flesh is granted for food (v.3), man may not eat it with the blood (the life) in it (the blood to be drained out before the flesh was eaten, not that the blood is itself the life, but that the life is in the blood, Leviticus 17:11, the one restriction on the grant of animal food); the prohibition resting on the blood being the seat of the life — 'the life thereof, which is the blood thereof' (the blood, as the vehicle of life, to be treated with reverence and not consumed, the prohibition teaching a reverence for life, which belongs to God, and restraining cruelty, the blood, later reserved for atonement, Leviticus 17:11, not to be eaten) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

Clarke and some hold the blood-prohibition a perpetual, moral ordinance, given to all mankind in Noah, and renewed in the apostolic decree (Acts 15:20, 29, 'abstain from blood and from things strangled'), binding still; others (with the reformed) hold it a positive/ceremonial precept, connected with the reverence for life and the use of blood in sacrifice, which under the gospel is no longer binding as a ceremonial law (the decree of Acts 15 a temporary accommodation). The force of the prohibition divides between a perpetual moral ordinance and an abrogated ceremonial precept.

debates.question: Is the prohibition of eating blood a perpetual moral ordinance or a ceremonial precept?

sides.position: A perpetual, moral ordinance given to all mankind in Noah, renewed in the apostolic decree (Acts 15:20, 29), binding still
A positive/ceremonial precept (reverence for life, blood reserved for sacrifice), abrogated under the gospel
holders: those who take Acts 15 as a temporary accommodation

themes: But flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat
the prohibition of eating blood (the crux of its meaning and force)
the life in the blood, reverence for the life that belongs to God
a perpetual moral ordinance (Acts 15:29) vs an abrogated ceremonial precept

schools.summary: God requires an account for blood: 'and surely your blood of your lives will I require; at the hand of every beast will I require it, and at the hand of man; at the hand of every man's brother will I require the life of man' — God requiring (calling to account) for the shedding of human blood (the life of man precious to God, and he avenging its unlawful taking, the sacredness of human life, which may not be shed without God requiring it); 'at the hand of every beast will I require it' (even a beast that kills a man required to answer, the beast that slew a man to be put to death, Exodus 21:28, the life of man so sacred that even an animal shedding it is held accountable, God's care for human life); 'at the hand of man... every man's brother will I require the life of man' (God requiring the life of man at the hand of the murderer, the man who sheds his brother's blood answering with his own, every man his brother's keeper, the murderer held accountable, the ground for the institution of capital justice, v.6) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: And surely your blood of your lives will I require; at the hand of every beast will I require it, and at the hand of man
God requiring an account for the blood of man
the sacredness of human life, its unlawful shedding avenged
every man his brother's keeper, the ground for capital justice

schools.summary: Gill and the dominant reading (the institution of capital justice): 'whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed' the institution of capital justice through human government — the murderer to be put to death 'by man,' that is, by the hand of the civil magistrate (the Targum: 'by the sentence of the judges'), God here establishing human government and the magistrate's authority to punish murder with death, the death penalty for murder divinely instituted, committed to man, the magistrate, to execute, the foundation of civil order and the sword of justice (Romans 13:4); 'for in the image of God made he man' grounding the sanctity of human life in the divine image (man, even fallen, still bearing the image of God, a real, abiding dignity, to shed man's blood being to assault God's image, the murder of a man so heinous, and its punishment so just, because man is made in God's image, the abiding image of God in man the basis of the sanctity of life and the severity of the penalty) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Ellicott.

debates.question: Is 'by man shall his blood be shed' the institution of capital justice or a prediction?

sides.position: The divine institution of judicial capital punishment through human government — the magistrate authorized to punish murder with death (the dominant reading; the Targum; Romans 13:4)
A prediction or general principle — the murderer will himself fall by violence (blood for blood)

themes: Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man
the law of capital justice, grounded in the image of God
the divine institution of capital punishment through the magistrate vs a prediction
the sanctity of life resting on the abiding image of God in man

schools.summary: The command repeated: 'and you, be ye fruitful, and multiply; bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein' — God repeating the command of v.1 (and of 1:28), Noah and his sons to be fruitful, multiply, and fill the earth abundantly (the renewed mandate of fruitfulness, after the digression on blood and justice, vv.4-6, God returning to the blessing of multiplication, the earth to be repeopled); the command, repeated, pressing the duty and the blessing of increase (the depopulated earth to be filled, the human race, through Noah's family, to multiply abundantly, God's purpose for the repopulation of the world); 'bring forth abundantly... and multiply therein' (the increase to be abundant, the earth to teem again with men, the renewed blessing of fruitfulness urging the multiplying of the race, the repopulation of the world through Noah's descendants, that the earth might be filled) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And you, be ye fruitful, and multiply; bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein
the repeated command to be fruitful and multiply
the renewed mandate of fruitfulness, the earth to be repeopled
the abundant increase repopulating the world through Noah

schools.summary: God speaks the covenant-word: 'and God spake unto Noah, and to his sons with him, saying' — God addressing Noah and his sons together, to establish his covenant with them (vv.9-17, the introduction to the covenant-promise, God speaking to the new heads of mankind, to assure them of his gracious engagement, the covenant made with Noah and his sons and their seed); God speaking to Noah 'and to his sons with him' (the covenant embracing not Noah only but his sons, and their seed, v.9, the whole family of the new humanity included, God's covenant of preservation extending to all mankind springing from Noah); 'saying' (God's covenant-word following, vv.9-11, God graciously condescending to make a covenant with Noah, his sons, and all living creatures, the assurance of God's promise that no flood would again destroy the earth, the covenant of God's faithfulness to the renewed creation) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And God spake unto Noah, and to his sons with him, saying
God's covenant-word to Noah and his sons
the covenant embracing the whole household and their seed
God graciously engaging himself to the new humanity

schools.summary: God establishes his covenant: 'and I, behold, I establish my covenant with you, and with your seed after you' — God himself, emphatically ('I, behold, I'), establishing (confirming, setting up) his covenant with Noah (the covenant God's own gracious act and engagement, the doubled 'I' marking the certainty and the divine initiative, God binding himself by covenant); 'my covenant' (the covenant God now establishes, the covenant of preservation, that no flood would again destroy the earth, v.11, God's gracious, unconditional promise, the covenant resting on God's faithfulness, not on man's merit); 'with you, and with your seed after you' (the covenant made with Noah and with his descendants, all mankind after him, embracing the whole human race springing from Noah, and, v.10, the living creatures, the covenant of preservation extending to all generations, God's faithfulness pledged to mankind and the creation for all time) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And I, behold, I establish my covenant with you, and with your seed after you
God establishing his covenant with Noah and his seed
the divine initiative, God binding himself by covenant
the covenant embracing all mankind after Noah, resting on God's faithfulness

schools.summary: The covenant with every living creature: 'and with every living creature that is with you, of the fowl, of the cattle, and of every beast of the earth with you; from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth' — the covenant embracing not only Noah and his seed but every living creature: the fowls, cattle, and beasts that came out of the ark, and all the beasts of the earth (the covenant of preservation extending to the whole animal creation, God's faithfulness pledged to all living things); the covenant including all the kinds of creatures preserved in the ark — fowls, cattle, beasts (the animal creation, saved through the flood, comprehended in the covenant, God's care extending to the creatures); 'from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth' (the covenant reaching all living creatures, those preserved in the ark and their descendants throughout the earth, the comprehensive scope of the covenant of preservation, God pledging that no flood would destroy the earth and its creatures again, the whole creation held in God's covenant faithfulness) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And with every living creature that is with you... from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth
the covenant extended to every living creature
the covenant of preservation embracing the whole animal creation
God's faithfulness pledged to all living things

schools.summary: The promise of no more flood: 'and I will establish my covenant with you; neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood; neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth' — the content of the covenant God's promise that all flesh shall not again be cut off by a flood, nor shall a flood again destroy the earth (God pledging never again to send such a universal deluge, the assurance of the world's preservation from another flood); all flesh never again cut off by a flood (the universal destruction by water not repeated, 8:21, God's faithful promise securing the creation against another deluge, the world resting on God's pledge); 'neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth' (no flood again destroying the earth, the promise sure and everlasting, v.16, though the world might fear another deluge, God's covenant forbidding it, the security of the earth from a flood-judgment resting on God's faithful word, the final judgment to be by fire, 2 Peter 3:7, not by water) — Gill, JFB, Wesley.

themes: neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood; neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth
the covenant promise that no flood shall again destroy the earth
God's faithful pledge securing the creation against another deluge
the world resting on God's covenant word

schools.summary: The token of the covenant: 'and God said, This is the token of the covenant which I make between me and you and every living creature that is with you, for perpetual generations' — God appointing a visible token or sign of his covenant (the rainbow, v.13, the sign confirming and sealing the promise, God graciously condescending to give a visible pledge of his word, the token the seal of the covenant); the covenant 'between me and you and every living creature' (God, Noah and his seed, and all living creatures, the token sealing the covenant made with the whole creation, the sign for the assurance of all, God's faithfulness visibly pledged to mankind and the creatures); 'for perpetual generations' (the token and the covenant for all generations, throughout the duration of the world, the sign a standing, perpetual pledge, every generation seeing the token and being assured of God's promise, the everlasting covenant, v.16, sealed by a perpetual sign) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: This is the token of the covenant which I make between me and you and every living creature that is with you, for perpetual generations
God appointing a token of the covenant for all generations
the visible sign sealing the covenant with the whole creation
the standing, perpetual pledge of God's promise

schools.summary: The rainbow appointed as the token: 'I do set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be for a token of a covenant between me and the earth' — Gill and the grammatical-historical holding the rainbow, a natural phenomenon, now appointed as the covenant-sign: 'I do set (I have set) my bow in the cloud' (the bow, the rainbow, formed by the natural causes — the sun shining on the cloud and rain — now constituted by God as the token of his covenant, God calling it 'my bow,' not because newly created, but because he appoints it to be a sign, the dominant view holding the rainbow existed in nature before but is here invested with new, covenantal significance; a second view holding the rainbow was now first created, that there had been no rain or no rainbow before the flood, and God now made the bow to appear as a new sign, but most, noting the natural causes, taking it as the existing phenomenon now consecrated as a sign); the bow fitly chosen, appearing in the cloud after rain, when men might fear a flood, speaking of God's promise (the bow, seen against the very cloud that brings rain, assuring that the rain will not become a flood); 'a token of a covenant between me and the earth' (the bow the sign of God's covenant with the earth, whenever it appears testifying to God's promise that no flood would destroy the earth, the visible pledge of God's faithfulness) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: Was the rainbow newly created here, or an existing phenomenon given new significance?

sides.position: The rainbow existed in nature (formed by sun and cloud) and is here invested with new covenantal significance (the dominant view)
The rainbow was now first created as a new sign (no rainbow before the flood)

themes: I do set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be for a token of a covenant between me and the earth
the rainbow appointed as the token of the covenant (the crux of the bow)
the natural rainbow invested with new covenantal significance vs a new creation
the bow, seen against the rain-cloud, assuring the world that no flood would destroy it

schools.summary: The bow appears in the cloud: 'and it shall come to pass, when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the bow shall be seen in the cloud' — whenever God brings a cloud (the rain-cloud) over the earth, the bow appearing in it (the rainbow seen against the cloud, when the sun shines on the falling rain, the very cloud that might bring fear of a flood bearing the bow of promise, the occasion of the sign's appearing); God himself bringing 'a cloud over the earth' (the clouds and rain under God's command, when he sends the rain-cloud, he also setting the bow in it, God governing both the cloud, which might fear, and the bow, which assures, the providence of God in the natural order); 'the bow shall be seen in the cloud' (the appearing of the bow in the rain-cloud the standing testimony of God's promise, whenever men see the bow against the cloud, they assured that the rain will not become a flood, the sign, regularly appearing, perpetually confirming the covenant, the bow seen the pledge remembered, v.15) — Gill.

themes: when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the bow shall be seen in the cloud
the bow appearing in the cloud
the rain-cloud bearing the bow of promise
the sign regularly appearing to assure the world that no flood would come

schools.summary: God remembers his covenant: 'and I will remember my covenant, which is between me and you and every living creature of all flesh; and the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh' — when the bow appears, God (speaking after the manner of men) 'remembering' his covenant (not that God forgets, he never forgets, but that the bow is the appointed token at which God, as it were, calls his promise to mind and acts on it, the sign assuring both God's faithful regard and man's confidence); God's 'remembering' spoken in human terms (the bow a reminder, not for God, who cannot forget, but as the seal of his sure promise, the language accommodating the divine to human understanding, God's faithfulness certain, and the bow sealing it); 'the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh' (the substance of the remembered covenant that the waters would never again become a flood to destroy all flesh, whenever the bow appears, the promise sealed afresh, the world assured that the rain will not become a deluge, God's faithful pledge securing all flesh from another flood) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And I will remember my covenant... and the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh
God remembering his covenant when he sees the bow
the bow the seal of God's sure promise (an anthropomorphism, not that God forgets)
the world assured against another deluge

schools.summary: The bow seals the everlasting covenant: 'and the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is upon the earth' — the bow standing in the cloud as the perpetual sign (God 'looking upon it,' an anthropomorphism, to 'remember' his everlasting covenant, the bow the standing seal of the covenant between God and all living creatures, the perpetual, visible pledge of God's faithfulness); God's 'looking upon' the bow and 'remembering' the covenant spoken in human terms (not that God needs a reminder, but that the bow is the appointed seal at which his promise is, as it were, called to mind, the language assuring man of God's faithful regard to his covenant); 'the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh' (the covenant everlasting, enduring as long as the earth, 8:22, embracing God and all living creatures, the bow sealing this perpetual covenant, God's faithfulness to the whole creation pledged forever, the standing bow testifying to the everlasting covenant) — Gill.

themes: And the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh
the bow as the seal of the everlasting covenant
the standing, visible seal of God's perpetual covenant with the creation
God's faithfulness to the whole creation pledged forever

schools.summary: God confirms the token to Noah: 'and God said unto Noah, This is the token of the covenant, which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the earth' — God repeating and confirming to Noah that the bow is the token of the covenant he has established (the repetition sealing the assurance, God confirming his gracious promise to Noah and all flesh, the covenant fixed and certain); God again naming the bow 'the token of the covenant' (the confirmation of the sign, the bow the appointed, certain pledge of God's word, the repeated declaration sealing the covenant to Noah's assurance); 'between me and all flesh that is upon the earth' (the covenant established between God and all flesh, all living creatures, the universal scope of the covenant of preservation, God's faithfulness pledged to the whole creation, the bow sealing God's promise to all flesh upon the earth) — Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: And God said unto Noah, This is the token of the covenant, which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the earth
God confirming the token of the covenant to Noah
the repeated confirmation sealing God's gracious promise
the universal scope of the covenant of preservation

schools.summary: The sons of Noah and Canaan: 'and the sons of Noah, that went forth of the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth: and Ham is the father of Canaan' — the three sons who came out of the ark named again (as the heads of the post-flood race, from these three the whole earth peopled, v.19, the genealogy turning to the three sons and their descendants, ch. 10, and to the incident of Noah's drunkenness and Ham's sin, vv.20-27); Shem, Ham, and Japheth the three from whom all nations would descend (the renewed humanity springing from these three, the line of promise continuing through Shem, v.26); 'and Ham is the father of Canaan' (the text noting that Ham was the father of Canaan, this added in anticipation of the curse on Canaan, v.25, the mention of Canaan preparing for the prophecy concerning him, the connection of Ham's sin, v.22, with the curse on his son Canaan, v.25, here foreshadowed) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the sons of Noah, that went forth of the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth: and Ham is the father of Canaan
the three sons of Noah, and that Ham was the father of Canaan
the three from whom the whole earth was peopled
the note of Canaan foreshadowing the prophecy (v.25)

schools.summary: The earth peopled from Noah's sons: 'these are the three sons of Noah: and of them was the whole earth overspread' — from the three sons of Noah (Shem, Ham, Japheth), the whole earth overspread (peopled), all the nations of the world descending from these three (the human race, reduced to eight souls, renewed and multiplied from Noah's sons, the unity of mankind in their descent from Noah); Shem, Ham, and Japheth the three heads of the post-flood race (the genealogy of the nations, ch. 10, tracing all peoples to these three, the whole human family springing from them); 'of them was the whole earth overspread' (the whole earth peopled from Noah's three sons, all the nations and races of mankind descending from this one family, the unity of the human race, Acts 17:26, 'of one blood all nations,' the repopulation of the world from the three sons of Noah) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: These are the three sons of Noah: and of them was the whole earth overspread
the whole earth peopled from Noah's three sons
all the nations descending from this one family
the unity of the human race in their descent from Noah (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: Noah plants a vineyard: 'and Noah began to be an husbandman, and he planted a vineyard' — Noah taking up the cultivation of the ground (he becoming a tiller of the soil, a farmer, after the flood Noah devoting himself to husbandry, tilling the renewed earth, the honest, useful labor of agriculture); Noah 'planted a vineyard' (among his husbandry, planting a vineyard and cultivating the vine, the planting of the vine a lawful, useful work, wine being a gift of God, Psalm 104:15, the vineyard yielding the wine of which Noah drank, v.21, the cultivation of the vine after the flood); the planting of the vineyard, in itself lawful and good, becoming the occasion of Noah's sin (v.21, not that the vine or wine is evil, but that Noah, perhaps not yet knowing its strength, or through carelessness, fell into drunkenness, the gift of God abused through intemperance, the occasion of the sad incident that follows) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Noah began to be an husbandman, and he planted a vineyard
Noah becoming a husbandman and planting a vineyard
the lawful cultivation of the vine, a gift of God (Psalm 104:15)
the vineyard becoming the occasion of Noah's sin

schools.summary: Noah's drunkenness: 'and he drank of the wine, and was drunken; and he was uncovered within his tent' — Noah drinking of the wine of his vineyard and becoming drunken (the sad fall of the righteous patriarch into intemperance, whether through ignorance of the wine's strength, perhaps a first experience of fermented wine, or through carelessness, Noah overcome, the sin of drunkenness recorded faithfully, even of a holy man); even Noah, just and perfect in his generations (6:9) and walking with God, falling into this sin (the Scripture not hiding the faults of its saints, Noah's drunkenness a warning of the frailty of the best of men and the danger of intemperance, the example of even the godly falling, that none might presume); 'he was uncovered within his tent' (in his drunkenness Noah lying uncovered, his nakedness exposed, within his tent, the drunkenness leading to his shameful, unseemly exposure, though within his tent, in private, his condition seen by Ham, v.22, the indecency that drunkenness brings, the occasion of Ham's sin) — Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And he drank of the wine, and was drunken; and he was uncovered within his tent
Noah's drunkenness and his uncovering within his tent
the sad fall of the righteous patriarch, a warning of human frailty
the shame and indecency that drunkenness brings

schools.summary: Ham's sin: 'and Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brethren without' — Ham seeing his father lying naked and drunk (the sin not the mere seeing, which might have been accidental, but his irreverent, unfilial response — he gazing without shame, and, instead of covering his father, going to mock, the dishonoring of his father, Ham's lack of reverence and natural affection); Ham, seeing his father's shame, not covering it or grieving, but looking on with irreverence and, it seems, delight (some traditions reading more into the act, but the text marking his unfilial, mocking spirit, Ham failing in the honor due to a father, his sin against the fifth commandment in spirit); 'told his two brethren without' (Ham going out and telling his two brothers, publishing his father's shame, instead of covering it spreading it, making his father's disgrace a matter of talk and mockery, the aggravation of his sin — not only seeing but publishing and deriding — the contrast with Shem and Japheth, who covered their father, v.23) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brethren without
Ham's sin in seeing and publishing his father's nakedness
his unfilial, mocking spirit, dishonoring his father
publishing and deriding his father's shame, in contrast to his brothers

schools.summary: Shem and Japheth cover their father: 'and Shem and Japheth took a garment, and laid it upon both their shoulders, and went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father; and their faces were backward, and they saw not their father's nakedness' — the two brothers taking a garment to cover their father (carrying it on both their shoulders, ready to lay it over him, their loving, reverent care to hide their father's shame, the contrast with Ham, who published it); they 'went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father' (walking backward toward their father, so as not to see his shame, and covering him, their delicate, modest care to honor their father even in his disgrace, the reverence and filial piety of Shem and Japheth); 'their faces were backward, and they saw not their father's nakedness' (turning their faces away, that they might not look on their father's shame, their studious avoidance of seeing what Ham had gazed upon, the honoring of their father, covering his fault in love, 1 Peter 4:8, 'charity shall cover the multitude of sins,' the pattern of filial reverence and of covering, rather than publishing, the faults of others) — Gill, JFB, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Shem and Japheth took a garment... and covered the nakedness of their father; and their faces were backward, and they saw not their father's nakedness
Shem and Japheth covering their father's nakedness with reverence
their filial piety, honoring their father in his shame, in contrast to Ham
the pattern of covering, not publishing, a fault (1 Peter 4:8)

schools.summary: Noah awakes and knows: 'and Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his younger son had done unto him' — Noah coming to himself, sobered from his drunkenness (he awaking and recovering his senses, the patriarch, recovered, learning what had passed during his stupor, the awaking that brought the knowledge of Ham's sin and his brothers' kindness); Noah 'knew what his younger son had done unto him' (Noah coming to know, whether by report or by the Spirit of prophecy, what Ham had done, 'his younger son' generally taken to mean Ham, though the term has prompted discussion of birth order, Noah learning of Ham's irreverence and his brothers' reverent covering); Noah, awaking, knowing Ham's sin, and, moved by the Spirit, pronouncing the prophetic curse and blessing (vv.25-27, the knowledge of what was done leading to the prophecy, Noah, as a patriarch and prophet, speaking not in mere personal resentment but by divine inspiration, foretelling the destinies of his sons' descendants, the awaking and knowledge the occasion of the prophecy) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his younger son had done unto him
Noah awaking and knowing what had been done to him
Noah recovered from his drunkenness, learning of Ham's sin
the knowledge moving Noah to pronounce, by inspiration, the prophetic curse and blessing

schools.summary: The curse on Canaan: 'and he said, Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren' — Ham having sinned (v.22), yet the curse falling on Canaan, Ham's son, several explanations offered: (1) the curse on Ham falling through his son Canaan (Ham, who dishonored his father, punished in his posterity, in the son perhaps dearest to him); (2) Canaan himself sharing in or prompting the sin (some traditions holding Canaan involved); (3) the curse prophetic of the Canaanites (Noah, by the Spirit, foretelling the degradation and subjection of Canaan's descendants, the Canaanites, who would be dispossessed and made servants by Israel, Shem's line, and others, Ham passed over, the prophecy fixing on Canaan, whose posterity's later wickedness and subjection are foretold; Clarke noting the Arabic version reads 'Ham the father of Canaan,' but this rejected); 'a servant of servants (the lowest, most abject servant) shall he be unto his brethren' (Canaan's descendants the most abject of servants, subject to the descendants of Shem and Japheth, the prophecy fulfilled in the subjugation of the Canaanites by Israel, Joshua, and their later servitude, 'of servants' a Hebrew superlative marking the depth of the subjection); the curse prophecy, not mere imprecation (Noah, by inspiration, foretelling, not arbitrarily decreeing, the destiny of Canaan's line, the curse answering to the later wickedness of the Canaanites, the verse concerning the Canaanites, not a racial curse on any people) — Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: Why is Canaan cursed rather than Ham, the offender?

sides.position: The prophecy fixes on the Canaanites — Noah foretells the degradation and subjection of Canaan's descendants to Shem's line, fulfilled in Israel's subjugation of the Canaanites
The curse on Ham falls through his son Canaan — Ham punished in his posterity (the son perhaps dearest to him)
Canaan himself shared in or prompted the sin

themes: And he said, Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren
Noah's curse on Canaan (the crux of why Canaan, not Ham)
the prophecy of the Canaanites' degradation and subjection to Shem's line
fulfilled in the subjugation of the Canaanites by Israel

schools.summary: Blessed be the LORD God of Shem: 'and he said, Blessed be the LORD God of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant' — Noah blessing not Shem directly, but the LORD (Jehovah) as the God of Shem (the blessing of Shem expressed as a blessing of God, who would be in a special covenant relation to Shem's line, the highest blessing — that the LORD would be Shem's God — the line of promise and the Messiah coming through Shem); the LORD being 'the God of Shem' marking Shem's posterity as the chosen line (the people of God, Israel, and the line of the promised seed descending from Shem, the covenant relation, the true religion, and the Messiah belonging to Shem's line, the spiritual blessing surpassing the temporal, God himself Shem's portion); 'and Canaan shall be his servant' (Canaan, the Canaanites, servant to Shem, Shem's descendants, Israel, the prophecy of the subjection of Canaan to Shem's line, fulfilled when Israel conquered and subjected the Canaanites, the curse on Canaan, v.25, repeated in relation to Shem's blessing, the line of promise exalted, the cursed line subjected) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he said, Blessed be the LORD God of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant
Noah blessing the LORD God of Shem
Shem's line the chosen, the line of the promise and the Messiah
Canaan subjected to Shem's line, fulfilled in Israel

schools.summary: The blessing of Japheth: 'God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant' — 'God shall enlarge Japheth' a wordplay (Japheth/yapht, 'enlarge'), God enlarging Japheth, giving his descendants a wide extent, a large possession (the posterity of Japheth, the peoples of Europe and the north/west, numerous and widely spread, the enlargement fulfilled in the great spread of his descendants over the earth); Gill noting the disputed question, who dwells in the tents of Shem: the dominant Christian reading taking Japheth as the subject — Japheth (the Gentiles, his descendants) dwelling in the tents of Shem, that is, the Gentiles brought into the spiritual blessings of Shem's line, into the church, sharing in the covenant and the Messiah who came of Shem (the calling of the Gentiles, Ephesians 2-3; Romans 11, Japheth dwelling in Shem's tents the Gentiles partaking of the gospel and the blessing of Abraham); a second reading taking God as the subject — 'God shall dwell in the tents of Shem' (God dwelling among Shem's posterity, in the tabernacle and temple, and in Christ of Shem's line, the Shekinah and the incarnation in Shem's line); 'and Canaan shall be his servant' (Canaan servant to Japheth also, as to Shem, v.26, the Canaanites subjected to both lines, the curse on Canaan repeated) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: Who shall dwell in the tents of Shem — Japheth, or God?

sides.position: Japheth (the Gentiles) — the Gentiles brought into the spiritual blessings of Shem's line, the calling of the Gentiles into the church (the dominant reading)
God — 'God shall dwell in the tents of Shem,' the Shekinah and the incarnation in Shem's line

themes: God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant
the blessing of Japheth (the crux of who dwells in the tents of Shem)
Japheth (the Gentiles) brought into the spiritual blessings of Shem's line vs God dwelling in Shem's tents
God enlarging Japheth, with Canaan servant to both lines

schools.summary: Noah's long life after the flood: 'and Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years' — Noah living three hundred and fifty years after the flood (the long life of the patriarch continuing into the renewed world, Noah seeing many generations of his descendants multiply and the earth repeopled, the bridge between the old world and the new living on for centuries); Noah's long life after the flood allowing him to see the growth of the nations from his sons (he living to witness the repopulation of the earth and, by the chronology, nearly to the time of Abraham, the long-lived patriarch's continuance in the renewed world); Noah's three hundred and fifty years after the flood making him a living witness, through many generations, of the flood and God's covenant (he able to testify to his descendants of the judgment and the deliverance, the patriarch's long life preserving the memory and the knowledge of God in the new world) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years
Noah's long life after the flood
the patriarch seeing the earth repeopled from his sons
a living witness of the flood and God's covenant through many generations

schools.summary: The death of Noah: 'and all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years: and he died' — the full span of Noah's life, nine hundred and fifty years (six hundred before the flood, three hundred and fifty after, among the longest-lived of the patriarchs, the great age of Noah, who bridged the old and new worlds); Noah's nine hundred and fifty years among the longest in Scripture (after Methuselah and Jared, the great age of the patriarch, spanning the flood and the early generations of the renewed world, the long life now drawing, like all, to its end); 'and he died' (like the patriarchs before the flood, ch. 5, Noah, the righteous and long-lived, dying at last, the sentence of death, 3:19, taking effect even on Noah, the refrain that tolled through the genealogy of ch. 5 sounding once more over Noah, the righteous patriarch, who walked with God and was saved through the flood, ending his long life in the grave, in hope of the resurrection) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: And all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years: and he died
the total of Noah's years and his death
the great life of the patriarch who bridged the old and new worlds
the refrain of mortality sounding once more over the righteous Noah
